response: In simple terms, gravity is the invisible, steady tug that every object with mass—from a crumb of bread to a blazing star, even your own body—exerts on every other object with mass. The more mass an object has, the stronger its tug: Earth’s enormous mass, for example, pulls hard enough to keep our feet planted on the ground, hold our breathable atmosphere in place, lock the moon into its looping orbit, and yank a dropped pencil straight down instead of letting it drift away. Smaller objects (like a single grain of sand) have such tiny gravitational tugs that we never notice them, but they’re always at work. That is gravity.
